ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Spanish mackerel vermicelli. FOODON_00002572 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — prepared fish dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Spanish mackerel vermicelli. A0CKP (prepared fish dishes) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Thai seafood noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of clear or lightly seasoned broth with a portion of poached or simmered Spanish mackerel and a nest of rehydrated mung bean vermicelli, garnished with spring onion and coriander.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated by component analysis: Spanish mackerel (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175136, Atlantic mackerel as proxy), mung bean vermicelli (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168874), and fish broth with aromatics. Weighted composite for a typical Thai mackerel vermicelli bowl (approximately 35% fish, 25% cooked glass noodles, 40% broth and aromatics).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Spanish mackerel vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from a weighted component model: Spanish mackerel (USDA FDC 175136, Atlantic mackerel as closest available proxy; Scomberomorus commerson is nutritionally similar), cooked mung bean glass noodles (USDA FDC 168874), and a clear fish broth base. Mackerel component contributes protein and fat; glass noodles contribute carbohydrate; broth contributes sodium. Per-100g values reflect the composite dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mung bean vermicelli / glass noodles GI approximately 33–39 (mean ~35); mackerel and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spanish mackerel vermicelli as a composite dish. GI of 35 is assigned based on the dominant glycemic contributor: mung bean glass noodles (vermicelli), which have a published GI of approximately 33–39 in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 international GI tables (Atkinson et al.). The fish and broth components do not contribute meaningfully to glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 34.0) / 100 = 11.9, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving ≈ 34.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving provides approximately 1680 mg sodium, representing 73–84% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit broth consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from mung bean glass noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The mung bean vermicelli base has a GI of approximately 35 (low), producing a moderate glycemic load of ~11.9 per serving, making this dish a relatively suitable carbohydrate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from Spanish mackerel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Spanish mackerel is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; however, the high sodium content from fish sauce warrants moderation for individuals with existing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spanish Mackerel Vermicelli is a Thai seafood noodle dish featuring poached or simmered Spanish mackerel (Scomberomorus commerson) served over rehydrated mung bean glass noodles (wun sen) in a clear, aromatic broth seasoned with fish sauce, garlic, coriander root, and white pepper. The dish is garnished with spring onion, fresh coriander, and sometimes celery. The texture contrasts the firm, flaky white flesh of the mackerel with the soft, slippery glass noodles. The broth is light and savoury with a clean fish flavour. Spanish mackerel is prized in Thai cuisine for its firm texture and rich flavour, and is a good source of high-quality protein, omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12, niacin, selenium, and vitamin D. The mung bean vermicelli contributes a low-GI carbohydrate base (GI ~35), making the overall dish relatively suitable for glycaemic management. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from fish sauce and broth, which can be significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai seafood restaurants and is associated with premium pricing due to the cost of fresh Spanish mackerel. It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Spanish mackerel vermicelli as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central entries for Atlantic mackerel (as a proxy for Spanish mackerel) and mung bean glass noodles. GI is estimated from published values for mung bean vermicelli. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model (approximately 35% Spanish mackerel, 25% cooked mung bean glass noodles, 40% clear fish broth and aromatics) using USDA FoodData Central as the approved source. Atlantic mackerel (USDA FDC 175136) is used as a proxy for Spanish mackerel (Scomberomorus commerson); both are oily fish with comparable macronutrient profiles. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in both fish and mung bean noodle components per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero by component analysis. 3. Sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects a typical Thai fish broth seasoned with fish sauce; actual sodium will vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on published mung bean glass noodle GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and are not directly reported from a single source entry for this dish. 6. This dish is served at Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 24.80. 7. Thai cuisine classification: mandatory Thai-language local name provided as วุ้นเส้นปลาอินทรี (Wun Sen Pla Inthri).
